topic: budgeting help — student loan repayment strategies

turns.content: I need help figuring out the best way to pay off my student loans because I feel like I've just been paying the minimum and getting nowhere

That's a really common frustration — minimum payments on student loans can feel like you're barely making a dent, especially with interest accruing. Let me help you think through this. First, do you know roughly how much you owe total and what your interest rates look like? And are these federal loans, private, or a mix?

ok so I have about 42k left, all federal. I think my interest rate is around 5.5% or 6% I'd have to check the exact number. I've been on the standard 10 year plan but I've been paying for like 4 years and it still feels like I owe the same amount

That's totally normal with the standard plan — the first several years, a big chunk of your payment goes to interest rather than principal, so the balance drops slowly at first and then accelerates later. The good news is $42k at that rate on a 10-year plan is very manageable. A few strategies to consider: you could stick with the standard plan and make extra payments toward principal when you can, you could look into income-driven repayment if cash flow is tight, or you could explore whether you qualify for Public Service Loan Forgiveness since you work in healthcare.
wait Public Service Loan Forgiveness?? I've heard of that but I always assumed I didn't qualify or it was one of those things that sounds too good to be true

It's real, and it's gotten much better in recent years after a lot of reforms. The basic requirement is: you work full-time for a qualifying employer — which includes nonprofit hospitals, government agencies, and most healthcare systems — and you make 120 qualifying monthly payments on an income-driven repayment plan. After those 120 payments, the remaining balance is forgiven tax-free. Depending on where you work, there's a good chance your employer qualifies.
120 payments so that's 10 years. I've already been paying for 4 years though so would those count??

They might! This is where it gets a little tricky. Your previous payments count only if you were on a qualifying repayment plan AND working for a qualifying employer during those months. The standard 10-year plan technically qualifies, but since you'd pay off the loan in 10 years anyway on that plan, there's usually nothing left to forgive. The real benefit comes from switching to an income-driven plan, which lowers your monthly payment, and then getting the rest forgiven after 120 payments. I'd strongly recommend submitting the PSLF Employment Certification Form to get an official count of how many qualifying payments you've made so far.
oh ok that makes sense. so if I switch to an income driven plan my monthly payment would go down but I'd pay for longer unless PSLF kicks in and forgives the rest at the 10 year mark?

Exactly. On an income-driven plan like SAVE, PAYE, or IBR, your payment is based on your income — usually 10-20% of discretionary income. So your monthly bill drops, you free up cash flow now, and then at the 120-payment mark the government forgives whatever's left. Without PSLF, income-driven plans also forgive after 20-25 years, but that forgiveness can be taxable. The PSLF route is almost always better if you qualify.
this is blowing my mind a little bit lol. ok so what should I actually do like step by step

Here's a practical game plan: First, go to studentaid.gov and check your exact loan balances, types, and interest rates. Second, submit the PSLF Employment Certification Form — you can do it online through the PSLF Help Tool. Third, if your employer qualifies, switch to an income-driven repayment plan through your loan servicer. Fourth, set a calendar reminder to recertify your income and employment annually. And fifth, keep paying every month — even the lower amount counts toward your 120 payments.
ok I'm literally writing this down right now. one more question, should I still try to make extra payments or is it better to just pay the minimum on the income driven plan and let PSLF do its thing

Great question. If you're going for PSLF, do NOT make extra payments. It sounds counterintuitive, but every extra dollar you pay is money that would have been forgiven. Pay the minimum required under your income-driven plan and put any extra money toward other financial goals — emergency fund, retirement contributions, or paying down any higher-interest debt. Let forgiveness do the heavy lifting.

wow ok that's wild but it makes total sense when you explain it. I've literally been throwing extra money at this loan for no reason. ok I'm going to go to studentaid.gov tonight and start this process. thank you so much this could save me like thousands of dollars

It really could — potentially tens of thousands depending on your balance at the forgiveness point. This is one of those situations where a little bit of paperwork now pays off massively later. Good luck with the application, and make sure to save copies of everything you submit. You've got this!
